summary:
Both prolactin and growth hormone are directly inhibited by hypothalamic neurotransmitters—prolactin by dopamine and growth hormone by somatostatin—which establishes their regulatory dependence on hypothalamic input. This reaffirms selection $\boxed{\text{D}}$. 

(Note: All other considerations such as potential stimulatory roles of TRH were acknowledged but do not override the well-established tonic inhibitory controls.)  

(End summary)

Only include content strictly related to the core medical facts about hormonal inhibition; avoid tangential details or speculative interpretations beyond what’s supported by retrieval results.

We are asked only to summarize Agent1's reasoning in exactly TWO SENTENCES focusing solely on KEY CLINICAL POINTS RELEVANT TO THE QUESTION.

Step-by-step extraction:
→ Identify central claim made: Hypothalamus regulates prolactin & GH through specific mechanisms.
→ Key supporting facts found in docs:
   • Pro-lactin: Inhibited by dopamine (tuberoinfundibular)
   • GH: Inhibited by somatostatin (growth-hormone-inhibiting hormone/GHIH)
→ These pathways originate within the hypothalamus and target ant Pituitary glands.
→ Minor nuance noted: TRH might increase prolactin pathophysiologically—but it doesn't invalidate main point of tonic inhibition.
→ Conclusion drawn: Correct answer is D ("both") because both hormones have clear, direct hypothalamic inhibition.

Now condense into just two clinically focused sentences without extra commentary.
